nodes.label: ZebraLogic Dataset
nodes.description: A benchmark testing LLM logical reasoning through constraint satisfaction problems involving spatial relations.
nodes.wiki: ZebraLogic is a benchmark that evaluates logical reasoning through constraint satisfaction problems inspired by the classic Zebra Puzzle, in which models must determine the unique assignment of attributes to entities satisfying a set of spatial and relational constraints expressed in natural language. The problems require systematic case analysis and constraint propagation, skills that formal solvers handle naturally but that challenge purely neural approaches. ZebraLogic is included in the VERGE benchmark suite as a representative of combinatorial constraint reasoning, where the SMT-based verification pipeline can provide strong formal guidance. Performance on ZebraLogic reflects a system's ability to translate relational natural language constraints into executable logical representations.

nodes.label: AR-LSAT Dataset
nodes.description: A dataset based on analytical reasoning questions from the LSAT exam, requiring complex legal and logical inference.
nodes.wiki: AR-LSAT is a dataset derived from the analytical reasoning section of the Law School Admission Test (LSAT), comprising problems that require complex logical inference over structured scenarios involving ordering, grouping, and selection constraints. The problems are notable for their difficulty and the multi-step conditional reasoning they demand, making them a challenging benchmark for both human test-takers and AI systems. Within the VERGE evaluation, AR-LSAT assesses the framework's ability to handle semi-formal reasoning tasks grounded in legal and procedural logic. Strong performance on AR-LSAT indicates robustness of the verification and refinement pipeline under conditions of high reasoning complexity and ambiguous natural language specification.

nodes.label: BBEH Dataset
nodes.description: Big-Bench Extra Hard, a challenging benchmark for evaluating complex reasoning capabilities of language models.
nodes.wiki: Big-Bench Extra Hard (BBEH) is a highly challenging reasoning benchmark derived from the BIG-Bench suite, designed to contain tasks that remain difficult even for state-of-the-art large language models after standard prompting and fine-tuning. The benchmark spans a diverse range of reasoning categories including mathematical, logical, and algorithmic tasks, providing a broad assessment of advanced reasoning capabilities. VERGE includes BBEH in its evaluation suite to demonstrate performance gains on tasks that push the boundaries of current LLM reasoning, beyond the more structured logical inference benchmarks. Results on BBEH reflect the generalization of the VERGE framework to heterogeneous and unpredictable reasoning challenges.

nodes.label: HLE Dataset
nodes.description: Humanity's Last Exam, an extremely challenging benchmark testing advanced reasoning across diverse academic domains.
nodes.wiki: Humanity's Last Exam (HLE) is an extremely challenging benchmark designed to test advanced reasoning across a wide variety of academic and professional domains, with problems sourced from expert-level examinations and competitions in mathematics, science, law, and other fields. HLE is intended to probe the upper limits of LLM reasoning capability, with problems that are difficult even for highly capable frontier models. Its inclusion in the VERGE evaluation suite serves to assess whether formal verification and iterative refinement provide measurable benefits at the frontier of model capability, where single-pass approaches already perform at high levels. Performance on HLE is a stringent test of whether the VERGE framework offers systematic gains beyond marginal cases.

nodes.label: Proof of Thought (PoT)
nodes.description: A neurosymbolic baseline that converts reasoning contexts into executable programs for single-pass logical inference.
nodes.wiki: Proof of Thought (PoT) is a neurosymbolic baseline system that converts natural language reasoning problems into structured intermediate representations, typically executable programs or logical expressions, which are then evaluated by an external interpreter or solver to derive answers. PoT operates in a single-pass fashion, generating the formal representation once and executing it without subsequent refinement based on verification feedback. In the VERGE paper, Proof of Thought is used as a comparison system to demonstrate the advantage of iterative refinement and targeted error localization over single-pass neurosymbolic translation. The contrast between PoT and VERGE highlights the importance of closed-loop verification feedback in improving reasoning accuracy.

nodes.label: Logic-LM
nodes.description: A neurosymbolic baseline that empowers LLMs with symbolic solvers for logical reasoning, used as a comparison system.
nodes.wiki: Logic-LM is a neurosymbolic framework that augments large language models with symbolic logic solvers to improve performance on structured logical reasoning tasks, translating natural language problems into formal logical representations that are solved by an external prover. The system represents an early and influential approach to combining the language understanding capabilities of LLMs with the deductive precision of symbolic AI. In the VERGE evaluation, Logic-LM serves as a comparison baseline to situate VERGE within the broader landscape of neurosymbolic reasoning systems. VERGE extends beyond Logic-LM's approach by introducing iterative refinement, semantic routing, and MCS-based error localization rather than relying on a single-pass translation and solve paradigm.

nodes.label: LINC
nodes.description: A neurosymbolic approach combining language models with first-order logic provers for logical reasoning tasks.
nodes.wiki: LINC (Logical Inference via Neurosymbolic Computation) is a neurosymbolic reasoning approach that combines large language models with first-order logic theorem provers to handle tasks requiring rigorous deductive inference. The system translates natural language premises and hypotheses into first-order logic and uses a prover to determine entailment, offering formal correctness guarantees where the translation is accurate. LINC is included as a baseline in the VERGE evaluation suite to represent the class of single-pass neurosymbolic systems that rely on first-order logic provers rather than SMT solvers. Comparing VERGE to LINC highlights the benefits of SMT-based verification with iterative refinement over static first-order logic proof search.

nodes.label: Verification Score
nodes.description: An aggregated score combining individual claim verification results with a variance-based penalty to measure answer quality.
nodes.wiki: The verification score is an aggregated quality metric used in VERGE to evaluate the overall logical consistency of an LLM-generated answer after claim decomposition and verification. It combines the proportion of individual atomic claims that pass verification with a variance-based penalty that discourages answers in which some claims are strongly verified while others remain in doubt, rewarding uniformly consistent responses. The verification score serves as the acceptance criterion in the iterative refinement loop, with answers accepted when their score exceeds a defined threshold or when further refinement fails to improve it. This metric provides a principled and interpretable measure of answer quality that goes beyond simple correctness labels.

nodes.label: Vikash Singh
nodes.description: First author of the VERGE paper, affiliated with Case Western Reserve University.
nodes.wiki: Vikash Singh is the first author of the paper introducing the VERGE framework, affiliated with Case Western Reserve University. His research contributions in the VERGE work span the design and implementation of the neurosymbolic pipeline, including the iterative refinement loop, semantic routing mechanism, and integration of SMT-based verification. Singh's background bridges natural language processing and formal methods, reflecting the interdisciplinary nature of the VERGE project. The paper represents a collaboration between his academic institution and researchers at Amazon Web Services.

nodes.wiki: Amazon Web Services (AWS) is the cloud computing subsidiary of Amazon.com and a major research institution in the fields of machine learning, natural language processing, and artificial intelligence. The majority of the authors of the VERGE paper are affiliated with AWS, where the primary research and development of the framework was conducted. AWS has a broad portfolio of AI research spanning foundation models, automated reasoning, and applied machine learning, situating VERGE within a broader organizational interest in reliable and verifiable AI systems. The collaboration between AWS researchers and academic partners at Case Western Reserve University reflects a common model of industry-academic partnership in contemporary AI research.

edges.description: Agent Contracts extend the Contract Net Protocol's task allocation metaphor to encompass resource-bounded execution governance.
Resource constraints R form the core multi-dimensional budget component of the Agent Contract seven-tuple.
Temporal constraints T are a required component of the Agent Contract tuple specifying duration boundaries.
Success criteria Φ define the fulfillment conditions within an Agent Contract.
Termination conditions Ψ specify events that end an Agent Contract regardless of fulfillment progress.
Agent Contracts introduce a formal lifecycle state machine governing transitions from DRAFTED through terminal states.
Agent Contracts establish conservation laws ensuring delegated sub-agent budgets respect parent contract constraints in hierarchical systems.
Agent Contracts operationalize Simon's satisficing principle by defining acceptable quality thresholds within explicit resource budgets.
Agent Contracts draw on contract algorithm theory to transform LLM agents into computation processes with pre-specified resource bounds.
Agent Contracts operationalize the normative MAS perspective by treating resource constraints as prohibitions and success criteria as obligations.
Hard enforcement is the structural enforcement layer of Agent Contracts that externally halts agents upon constraint violation.
Budget-aware prompting is the soft enforcement mechanism within Agent Contracts that communicates remaining resources to agents.
Hard enforcement provides strict structural guarantees that complement soft budget-aware prompting, which agents may ignore due to token elasticity.
Agent Contracts introduce configurable contract modes (URGENT, ECONOMICAL, BALANCED) that trade resource consumption for output quality.
Agent Contracts apply to orchestrator-worker patterns by formalizing subcontract delegation with conservation law enforcement.
Conservation laws constrain orchestrator-worker patterns by ensuring the sum of subcontract allocations does not exceed the parent contract budget.
The TALE framework identifies and documents the token elasticity phenomenon where LLMs exceed specified budgets under tight constraints.
Token elasticity motivates hard structural enforcement since prompt-only soft enforcement is insufficient for strict budget compliance.
Agent Contracts improve upon TALE by adding structural hard enforcement beyond prompt-based budget communication.
Agent Contracts address similar budget adherence goals as BudgetThinker but operate at the orchestration layer rather than requiring model-level control tokens.
SelfBudgeter predicts required budgets while Agent Contracts formally specify and enforce budgets as governance constraints.
Agent Contracts are validated on the Code Review experiment demonstrating 90% token reduction and 525× variance reduction.
Agent Contracts are validated on the Research Pipeline experiment demonstrating zero conservation law violations.
The Code Review experiment uses 70 problems from LiveCodeBench released after model knowledge cutoff to avoid contamination.
Contract modes operationalize satisficing by offering predefined quality-resource tradeoff configurations aligned with Simon's bounded rationality theory.
Token budget decomposition into input, reasoning, and output categories enables fine-grained monitoring of the token resource constraint dimension.
Agent Contracts are compared to AutoGen, which provides operational controls but lacks formal resource governance mechanisms.
Agent Contracts are compared to LangGraph, which provides state management and observability but no formal budget conservation laws.
Agent Contracts are compared to MetaGPT, which structures agent interactions through workflow patterns without formal resource governance.
ReAct-style agents that synergize reasoning and acting are a primary target architecture for governance through Agent Contracts.
BATS demonstrates that explicit budget awareness in tool selection improves agent performance, supporting the importance of formal resource constraints.
MiCP is built upon conformal prediction theory to provide formal coverage guarantees in multi-turn LLM settings.
MiCP uses split conformal prediction to separate calibration from model training for threshold estimation.
MiCP is applied to adaptive RAG pipelines to enable principled early stopping during iterative retrieval.
MiCP is applied to ReAct-style agents to provide coverage guarantees in reasoning-and-acting multi-turn loops.
MiCP introduces adaptive early stopping as a core component to reduce unnecessary reasoning turns.
MiCP introduces multi-level error budget allocation to distribute the total alpha across turns while preserving end-to-end coverage.
MiCP uses conformal retrieval threshold calibration to filter low-relevance passages at each retrieval turn.
MiCP incorporates the Can't Answer label for questions whose correct answer never appears in any sampled responses.
MiCP introduces a composite metric to jointly evaluate coverage validity and inference efficiency in multi-turn settings.
MiCP uses normalized entropy of sampled answer distributions as the per-turn uncertainty signal for stopping decisions.
MiCP uses answer clustering to group semantically equivalent sampled outputs and compute frequency-based nonconformity scores.
MiCP uses grid search to optimize per-turn error budget allocations by minimizing the composite metric on a validation set.
The multi-level error budget allocation ensures the overall 1-alpha coverage guarantee is preserved even with adaptive early stopping.
MiCP extends the Can't Answer mechanism from TRAQ to multi-turn LLM interactions with adaptive stopping.
Retrieval filtering with conformal thresholds is a stage in MiCP that contributes to the overall coverage guarantee by preserving gold passages.
Normalized entropy serves as the uncertainty signal that drives the early stopping decision at each turn in MiCP.
The prediction set constructed by MiCP is calibrated to satisfy the 1-alpha marginal coverage guarantee.
MiCP is evaluated on HotpotQA in both adaptive RAG and ReAct settings to assess multi-hop reasoning coverage and efficiency.
MiCP is evaluated on Natural Questions as a single-hop benchmark to measure coverage and turn reduction.
MiCP is evaluated on TriviaQA to assess its performance on single-hop reading comprehension tasks.
MiCP is evaluated on MuSiQue to test its coverage and efficiency on complex multi-hop questions.
MiCP is evaluated on 2WikiMultiHopQA, where it achieves particularly strong early-stopping efficiency.
MiCP is evaluated using Gemma-2-9B-IT as a backbone LLM across all QA benchmarks.
MiCP is evaluated using Qwen3.5-9B as a backbone LLM, showing strong optimization via grid search.
MiCP is evaluated using GPT-4o-mini as a backbone LLM to demonstrate cross-model generalizability.
Nonconformity scores are the fundamental building block of conformal prediction used to calibrate coverage thresholds.
Answer clustering enables frequency-based nonconformity scores for LLMs that lack access to internal logits.
The composite metric is specifically designed to evaluate the quality of early stopping decisions in multi-turn reasoning.
Adaptive RAG and ReAct are two distinct multi-turn paradigms both supported and compared within the MiCP evaluation framework.
Compiled AI builds on DSPy's compilation paradigm of converting declarative LLM calls into optimized pipelines as a prior art antecedent.
Compiled AI extends the LLM+P hybrid approach of separating LLM intent understanding from deterministic execution to enterprise workflow contexts.
The paper introduces the Code Foundry architecture as the concrete system implementation of the compiled AI paradigm.
The four-stage validation pipeline is a mandatory component of the Code Foundry architecture that every generated artifact must pass before deployment.
The Code Foundry architecture produces validated Temporal workflow activities as its output artifacts.
The Code Foundry integrates a three-gate security pipeline protecting against OWASP LLM Top 10 vulnerabilities.
Compiled AI is evaluated on the Berkeley Function-Calling Leaderboard with 400 instances to validate the core compilation-vs-runtime trade-off.
Compiled AI is evaluated on DocILE with 5,680 invoice documents to stress-test the architecture on noisy semantic inputs.
Compiled AI is compared to AutoGen as a runtime agent baseline, achieving 84x token reduction at 1,000 transactions.
Compiled AI is compared to LangChain as a runtime orchestration baseline, achieving 57-77x token reduction advantage.
Compiled AI is compared to Direct LLM inference, achieving 57x token reduction and 450x latency improvement on function-calling tasks.
Compiled AI's one-time generation cost model enables dramatic token amortization, breaking even with runtime inference at approximately 17 transactions.
Compiled AI achieves 100% output reproducibility and zero entropy compared to 18-75% output variance in runtime inference systems.
The Code Factory variant is evaluated on DocILE, achieving the highest LIR score of 80.4% and matching Direct LLM on KILE at 80.0%.
Code Factory implements bounded agentic invocation by compiling task-specific LLM calls as code artifacts rather than constructing prompts per-request.
Code Factory achieves higher LIR accuracy (80.4% vs 74.5%) and 2.3x lower latency than Direct LLM on DocILE document intelligence.
Security analysis is the first stage of the four-stage validation pipeline, applying static analysis for SQL injection, command injection, and other vulnerabilities.
The Input Gate of the three-gate security pipeline uses DeBERTa-v3-based prompt injection detection achieving 95.8% recall with 100% precision.
Compiled AI is particularly suited to healthcare administrative workflows requiring determinism, auditability, and HIPAA compliance for prior authorization and clinical document processing.
The prior authorization workflow example uses bounded agentic invocation to confine LLM to structured clinical factor extraction while deterministic code makes coverage decisions.
Compiled AI is evaluated using total cost of ownership analysis showing 40x cost reduction at 1M transactions/month compared to Direct LLM.
DocILE uses KILE as one of its two evaluation metrics for measuring exact-match accuracy on key invoice fields.
DocILE uses LIR as one of its two evaluation metrics for measuring accuracy on structured line items within invoice documents.
Compiled AI combines constrained generation with multi-stage validation as a complementary approach to SynCode's grammar-constrained decoding for improving code reliability.
MetaGPT's Standard Operating Procedures represent an antecedent to compiled AI's deterministic workflow approach, reducing cascading hallucinations through procedural constraints.
AlphaCodium's multi-stage verification flows validate the principle of iterative validation in code generation that compiled AI's validation pipeline builds upon.
Prior authorization is a specific healthcare workflow exemplifying where compiled AI's determinism, auditability, and compliance properties are regulatory requirements.
Steffen Herbold is a co-author who introduces the formal uncertainty framework for LLM text generation.
Florian Lemmerich is a co-author who introduces the formal uncertainty framework for LLM text generation.
Steffen Herbold is affiliated with the University of Passau.
Florian Lemmerich is affiliated with the University of Passau.
The formal framework is based on the sampling tree as its core mathematical structure for representing LLM generation processes.
The framework introduces filter functions to specify which parts of the sampling tree are used for uncertainty computation.
The framework introduces objective functions to define the property with respect to which uncertainty is measured.
The prompting stage is the first phase modeled within the sampling tree, capturing uncertainty in prompt generation.
The decoding stage is the second phase modeled within the sampling tree, capturing token generation uncertainty.
The interpretation stage is the third phase modeled within the sampling tree, capturing uncertainty in output judgment.
Syntactic equality is the simplest family of objective functions that measures exact token sequence identity.
Hard clustering is a family of objective functions that groups sequences into discrete semantic clusters.
Soft clustering is a family of objective functions based on continuous similarity measures between sequences.
Monte Carlo simulation is formalized as a special estimation filter within the framework.
Top-k sampling is formalized as an estimation filter that restricts the sampling tree to the k most probable tokens.
The probability mass filter in the framework is directly based on the nucleus sampling strategy.
Objective-weighted probability is computed by applying the objective function to weight probabilities across filtered sequences.
Objective-weighted probability uses filter functions to restrict which sequences contribute to the computation.
Objective-weighted entropy is computed using objective-weighted probabilities as its underlying probability measure.
The formal framework generalizes and unifies semantic entropy by expressing it as a hard-clustering objective over the sampling tree.
Semantic entropy methods are naturally expressed using hard clustering objective functions within the framework.
Kernel language entropy and similarity-based entropy variants are expressed as soft clustering objectives in the framework.
Self-consistency methods are expressed within the framework using prefix filters over sets of prompt variations combined with syntactic or semantic objectives.
The framework subsumes kernel language entropy as a special case of soft-clustering objective-weighted entropy.
LLM-as-a-judge is identified as a key source of interpretation uncertainty that the framework can formally model.
Hallucinations are identified as a direct consequence of the stochastic uncertainty inherent in the LLM decoding process.
Aleatoric uncertainty in LLMs is manifested in the irreducible randomness of the decoding process.
Epistemic uncertainty arises partly from unclear prompt goals and the knowledge gaps of actors formulating prompts.
The framework provides formal tools to understand hallucinations as a consequence of uncertainty in the LLM generation process.
Monte Carlo simulation is the de facto standard estimator used by existing methods to approximate objective-weighted entropy.
The framework can represent aleatoric uncertainty through the inherent stochasticity modeled in the sampling tree.
The framework can represent epistemic uncertainty through model parameter uncertainty as an extension of the sampling tree.
Top-k sampling and nucleus sampling are contrasted as estimation filters, with nucleus sampling providing probability mass guarantees that top-k lacks.
Soft and hard clustering objectives are formally related within the framework, differing in whether sequence similarity is binary or continuous.
Every compiled AI artifact must pass through the four-stage validation pipeline (Security, Syntax, Execution, Accuracy) before deployment.
The compiled AI system produces validated Temporal activities as its output artifacts for deterministic workflow execution.
Code Factory is a variant of compiled AI that wraps bounded LLM calls as compiled code artifacts to handle semantic tasks requiring contextual understanding.
Code Factory matches Direct LLM on KILE accuracy (80.0%) while achieving higher LIR (80.4%) at 2.3x lower latency on DocILE.
Compiled AI outperforms Direct LLM on token efficiency (57x reduction), latency (450x improvement), and cost (40x TCO reduction) while matching accuracy on function-calling tasks.
The Security stage of the four-stage validation pipeline incorporates static analysis tools (Bandit, Semgrep) as part of the broader three-gate security architecture.
The Input Gate of the three-gate security architecture uses DeBERTa-v3-based prompt injection detection achieving 95.8% recall with 100% precision.
Prior authorization for GLP-1 agonists is a concrete healthcare workflow example demonstrating compiled AI with bounded agentic invocation for clinical data extraction.
Bounded agentic invocation confines LLM calls to structured clinical factor extraction within the prior authorization workflow while keeping coverage decisions deterministic.
Token amortization is a primary evaluation metric for compiled AI, with break-even at approximately 17 transactions and 57x savings at 1,000 transactions.
Compiled AI achieves perfect determinism (H=0, 100% reproducibility) compared to 95% for runtime inference, a key operational advantage.
DocILE uses KILE as its primary metric for measuring exact-match accuracy on key invoice fields like invoice number, date, and total amount.
DocILE uses LIR as a secondary metric for measuring accuracy on structured line items within invoice documents.
Compiled AI uses Claude Opus 4.5 at temperature 0 for the one-time code generation step during the compilation phase.
SynCode provides a complementary constrained generation approach that compiled AI's architecture combines with multi-stage validation for reliable code production.
MetaGPT's Standard Operating Procedures represent a related compiled workflow approach that constrains agent behavior, serving as prior work context for compiled AI.
AlphaCodium's structured multi-stage verification approach demonstrates GPT-4 accuracy improvements that validate the compilation paradigm adopted by compiled AI.
XY.AI Labs researchers developed and published the compiled AI paradigm, system architecture, and evaluation framework.
AutoReal is the methodology that is instantiated by fine-tuning Qwen2.5-Coder-7B to produce AutoReal-Prover as its concrete implementation.
CoT-based proof training is one of the two key methodological components of the AutoReal framework.
Context augmentation is one of the two key methodological components of the AutoReal framework.
AutoReal-Prover is obtained by fine-tuning Qwen2.5-Coder-7B using the CoT-based proof training procedure.
AutoReal-Prover is evaluated on 660 theorems from seL4 Important Theories across all 10 proof categories.
AutoReal-Prover is evaluated on three security-related AFP projects to assess generalization beyond seL4.
AutoReal-Prover's performance is measured using proof success rate as the primary evaluation metric.
CoT-based proof training uses the constructed step-level proof CoT dataset of approximately 200k instances from seL4.
CoT-based proof training is grounded in chain-of-thought reasoning by introducing intermediate reasoning traces aligned with proof steps.
CoT-based proof training is realized via instruction tuning of the base model on structured proof CoT training instances.
The proof CoT dataset is constructed from proof traces extracted by FVELER, which records seL4 proof developments as step-by-step sequences with proof states.
The proof CoT dataset is constructed from seL4 proof traces, containing step-level instances from the seL4 verification project.
Context augmentation supplies theorem-relevant proof context consisting of auxiliary definitions and lemmas to the LLM during proof generation.
AutoReal-Prover achieves a 51.67% proof success rate on seL4, substantially outperforming Selene's best result of 27.06% using GPT-4.
Selene relies on the closed-source GPT-4 model for LLM-driven theorem proving on seL4.
Selene evaluates LLM-driven theorem proving on 340 sampled seL4 theorems, reporting the previously best result of 27.06%.
FVELER investigates the application of LLMs to discharging proofs in the seL4 verification project.
AutoReal-Prover is evaluated on all 64 theorems in the CRYSTALS-Kyber_Security AFP project to assess generalization.
AutoReal-Prover is evaluated on all 278 theorems in the RSAPSS AFP project to assess generalization.
AutoReal-Prover is evaluated on all 109 theorems in the Elliptic_Curves_Group_Law AFP project to assess generalization.
AutoReal advances the application of LLM-driven theorem proving within the broader context of formal methods for industrial-scale system verification.
AutoReal generates Isabelle/HOL proof scripts that are validated by the Isabelle proof checker.
The seL4 microkernel verification is carried out entirely in Isabelle/HOL through a refinement-based proof methodology.
LEGO-Prover evaluates on the miniF2F benchmark and advances state-of-the-art proof success rates on this mathematics-oriented dataset.
ProofAug focuses on improving proof success rates on the miniF2F benchmark through fine-grained proof structure analysis.
Seed-Prover targets the miniF2F benchmark and represents a state-of-the-art result in mathematics-oriented theorem proving.
AutoReal extends LLM-driven theorem proving from benchmark-oriented datasets like miniF2F to real-world industrial-scale verification projects.
CRYSTALS-Kyber_Security is one of three security-related AFP projects selected for evaluating AutoReal-Prover's generalization.
RSAPSS is one of three security-related AFP projects selected for evaluating AutoReal-Prover's generalization.
Elliptic_Curves_Group_Law is one of three security-related AFP projects selected for evaluating AutoReal-Prover's generalization.
AutoReal-Prover is a compact 7B local model that outperforms prior work relying on closed-source GPT-4 for seL4 theorem proving.
Together, CoT-based proof training and context augmentation combine to improve both proof success rates and explainability of generated proofs.
FoVer is the framework used to synthesize and create the FoVer-40K training dataset.
FoVer leverages formal verification tools to assign accurate step-level error labels to reasoning traces.
FoVer uses Z3 to verify step-level correctness in formal logic (logical entailment) tasks.
FoVer uses Isabelle/HOL to verify step-level correctness in formal theorem proving tasks.
FoVer-40K includes formal logic reasoning data sourced from the FLDx2 dataset.
FoVer-40K includes theorem proving data derived from GSM8K-level math word problems.
FoVer-PRMs are obtained by fine-tuning Llama 3.1 8B or Qwen 2.5 7B on the FoVer-40K dataset.
One variant of FoVer-PRM is created by fine-tuning Llama 3.1 8B on FoVer-40K.
One variant of FoVer-PRM is created by fine-tuning Qwen 2.5 7B on FoVer-40K.
FoVer-PRM is a specific instantiation of the broader Process Reward Model paradigm.
FoVer-40K contains step-level error labels annotated on formal logic reasoning tasks.
FoVer-40K contains step-level error labels annotated on formal theorem proving tasks.
Z3 is used to verify solutions to first-order logical entailment tasks in FoVer.
Isabelle/HOL is used to verify formal proofs generated for math word problems.
FoVer-PRMs demonstrate formal-to-informal transfer by improving on informal reasoning tasks after training on formally verified data.
FoVer-PRMs exhibit cross-task generalization, improving on tasks substantially different from their training data.
FoVer-PRMs are evaluated against RLHFlow PRMs as existing baselines trained with Monte Carlo roll-outs.
FoVer-PRMs are compared to Qwen2.5-Math-PRM-7B, an existing PRM trained with MC roll-outs and LLM-as-a-judge.
FoVer is presented as a more efficient and accurate alternative to Monte Carlo roll-outs for PRM training data creation.
FoVer is presented as a cheaper and scalable alternative to human annotation for step-level error labeling.
FoVer-PRMs are evaluated using the Best-of-K methodology across multiple reasoning benchmarks.
FoVer-PRMs are evaluated on ProcessBench for step-level binary classification performance using AUROC.
FoVer-PRMs are evaluated on BIG-Bench Hard to assess cross-task generalization on unseen reasoning tasks.
FoVer's step-level verification is implemented by adapting formal verification tools to assess individual reasoning steps independently.
FoVer-Llama3.1-8B-PRM achieves better performance than RLHFlow PRMs on the majority of unseen task benchmarks.
AUROC is used as the evaluation metric for step-level classification performance on ProcessBench.
FoVer-40K is constructed by applying step-level formal verification to annotate error labels on LLM-generated reasoning traces.
Llama 3.1 8B is used to generate step-by-step formal solutions that are included in the FoVer-40K dataset.
Qwen 2.5 7B is used to generate step-by-step formal solutions that are included in the FoVer-40K dataset.
ProbGuard is designed specifically to monitor and enforce safety for LLM-powered autonomous agents during runtime execution.
ProbGuard learns a DTMC from execution traces to model agent behavioral dynamics and estimate probabilities of reaching unsafe states.
ProbGuard applies domain-specific predicate abstraction to map concrete agent states to finite symbolic states for probabilistic modeling.
ProbGuard encodes domain safety properties in CTL to enable probabilistic model checking and branching-time reasoning over agent futures.
ProbGuard incorporates PAC analysis to provide statistical guarantees that the learned DTMC accurately approximates the true agent dynamics.
ProbGuard applies valid-transition-aware Laplace smoothing to handle sparse transition data when learning the DTMC from execution traces.
ProbGuard uses PRISM to evaluate the symbolic DTMC model against CTL specifications and compute violation probabilities at runtime.
ProbGuard is implemented on top of the LangChain agent framework, integrating risk alerts into the agent's planning loop via prompt augmentation.
ProbGuard extends traditional reactive runtime monitoring with proactive probabilistic risk prediction to anticipate violations before they occur.
ProbGuard is empirically compared to AgentSpec in embodied agent tasks, showing 12.05% average token reduction and automated safety specification.
ProbGuard is compared to REDriver in autonomous driving scenarios, demonstrating superior advance warning times and interpretable probabilistic risk scores.
ProbGuard is evaluated in the autonomous driving domain using the Apollo simulator, providing warnings up to 38.66 seconds before traffic violations.
ProbGuard is evaluated on embodied household agent tasks, reducing unsafe behaviors by up to 65.37% while preserving 80.4% task completion.
ProbGuard uses AWT as a key effectiveness metric to measure how far in advance it predicts safety violations before they physically occur.
The DTMC is constructed over symbolic states defined by predicate abstraction, with transition probabilities estimated from empirical execution trace data.
LawBreaker formalizes traffic laws using a bounded fragment of Signal Temporal Logic to specify autonomous vehicle safety properties.
ProbGuard systematically translates LawBreaker's STL fragment into CTL by introducing auxiliary monitor automata that track bounded response obligations.
The autonomous driving evaluation derives safety properties and predicates from LawBreaker's formal traffic law specifications.
ProbGuard's autonomous driving evaluation is conducted using the Apollo autonomous driving simulator integrated with the monitoring framework.
Law-violating test scenarios for autonomous driving evaluation are adopted from the μDrive framework for generating diverse traffic violations.
The embodied agent evaluation uses SafeAgentBench to derive object- and state-level safety rules and define unsafe behavior predicates.
The embodied agent evaluation employs the ReAct framework in conjunction with SafeAgentBench's low-level controller for household manipulation tasks.
Both ProbGuard and ShieldAgent address LLM agent safety monitoring, but ProbGuard uses learned probabilistic models while ShieldAgent uses Markov logic networks.
Both ProbGuard and GuardAgent are safety enforcement frameworks for LLM agents, but ProbGuard takes a proactive probabilistic approach versus GuardAgent's reactive reasoning.
Haoyu Wang is the primary author who developed and introduced the ProbGuard framework.
Haoyu Wang is a PhD student at Singapore Management University where the ProbGuard research was conducted.
PAC analysis is applied to bound the deviation between learned DTMC transition probabilities and true system dynamics under finite sampling.
PRISM evaluates the symbolic DTMC model against CTL specifications to compute probabilities that safety properties are satisfied.
The framework is centered on the formal definition of execution context as the foundation for all security property evaluations.
The framework introduces task alignment as one of the four core security properties required for contextual authorization.
The framework introduces action alignment as one of the four core security properties required for contextual authorization.
The framework introduces source authorization as one of the four core security properties required for contextual authorization.
The framework introduces data isolation as one of the four core security properties required for contextual authorization.
The framework introduces oracle functions as ideal specifications defining what information is theoretically required to verify security properties.
The instruction attribution function I is one of the five oracle functions defined to enable security property verification.
The source attribution function L is one of the five oracle functions defined to enable security property verification.
The objective evaluation functions Hp, HTr, and Ha are oracle functions that quantify task objectives at different granularities.
Verifying source authorization requires the instruction attribution function to identify which inputs caused an action.
Verifying source authorization requires the source attribution function to determine the provenance of identified instruction inputs.
Source authorization checks whether instruction sources are members of authenticated sources as defined by the permission graph structure.
Task alignment is verified using Hp to extract the authorized objective and HTr to evaluate whether the trajectory serves that objective.
Action alignment is verified using Ha to evaluate whether each individual action serves the authorized objective in context of the trajectory.
Data isolation verification uses source attribution to determine the provenance of information contained in action arguments.
Data isolation checks whether information flows from source to destination are permitted by edges in the source permission graph R.
Indirect prompt injection is redefined as requiring a violation of source authorization, where instructions originate from unauthenticated external sources.
Indirect prompt injection additionally requires a violation of action alignment, where the resulting action does not serve the authorized objective.
Direct prompt injection is formalized as a task alignment violation where the user-requested objective conflicts with higher-priority system-defined constraints.
Jailbreaking is formalized as a task alignment violation where the user objective falls outside the allowed objective space O defined by safety alignment.
Task drift is formalized as a task alignment violation where HTr returns 0, indicating the trajectory has autonomously diverged from the authorized objective.
Memory poisoning involves injection from unauthenticated sources into agent memory, ultimately causing source authorization violations in future interactions.
Confused deputy attacks are formalized as source authorization violations where the agent executes actions the commanding user lacks permission to authorize.
Capability misuse is formalized as an action alignment violation where Ha=0 despite the agent having permission to perform the action.
Cross-context information leakage is formalized as a data isolation violation when information flows between sources lacking permission edges in R.
Prevention defenses are systematized as mechanisms that strengthen approximations of oracle functions through prompt engineering, training, and policy specification.
Detection defenses such as TaskShield and circuit breaking implement approximations of task alignment property checks.
Detection defenses such as input filtering and DataSentinel attempt to verify source authorization but lack instruction attribution capability.
RLHF and Constitutional AI training techniques establish the allowed objective space O that task alignment properties depend on.
Empirical results from AgentDojo are cited to corroborate the framework's predictions about which oracle approximations most effectively reduce attack success rates.
The framework provides a more precise contextual definition of indirect prompt injection than prior work, requiring both source authorization and action alignment violations.
LLM agent security requires a source permission graph to formalize which sources have permission to access resources and trigger agent actions.
The framework identifies compositional security as an open challenge where individually secure actions combine to create violations through environment state accumulation.
Memory poisoning also violates data isolation by allowing information from unauthenticated sources to persist and influence future authorized contexts.
Detection defenses including output filtering and tool filtering approximate action alignment checking but lack provenance information from oracle functions.
VERGE integrates SMT solvers as the formal verification backend for logical and mathematical claims.
VERGE uses LLMs for answer generation, claim decomposition, classification, autoformalization, and soft verification.
VERGE introduces the application of MCS computation to generate actionable feedback for LLM reasoning refinement.
VERGE introduces semantic routing to direct different claim types to appropriate verification strategies.
VERGE introduces semantic equivalence checking for robust multi-model consensus on autoformalized claims.
Z3 is the specific SMT solver implementation used within VERGE's verification pipeline.
VERGE employs iterative refinement to repeatedly improve LLM answers based on formal verification feedback.
VERGE decomposes LLM answers into atomic claims as a prerequisite step for individual claim verification.
VERGE uses autoformalization to translate natural language claims into SMT-LIB2 logical formulas.
MCS-based feedback transforms binary failure signals into actionable guidance, improving the effectiveness of iterative refinement.
Semantic routing directs commonsense and vague claims to soft verification using LLM ensembles.
Semantic routing directs mathematical and logical claims to formal SMT-based verification.
VERGE detects and corrects LLM hallucinations by identifying logically inconsistent claims through formal verification.
VERGE is evaluated on the FOLIO dataset for first-order logic reasoning performance.
VERGE is evaluated on ProofWriter to assess deductive reasoning capabilities.
VERGE is evaluated on ZebraLogic for constraint satisfaction and spatial reasoning tasks.
VERGE is evaluated on AR-LSAT for complex legal and analytical reasoning.
VERGE is evaluated on BBEH for challenging multi-domain reasoning tasks.
VERGE is evaluated on HLE for advanced academic reasoning across diverse domains.
VERGE is benchmarked against self-consistency prompting as a probabilistic baseline.
VERGE is compared against chain-of-thought prompting as a single-pass baseline.
VERGE is compared to Proof of Thought, which outperforms VERGE on the synthetic ProofWriter dataset.
VERGE is compared to Logic-LM as a neurosymbolic baseline across reasoning benchmarks.
VERGE is compared to LINC as a neurosymbolic first-order logic reasoning baseline.
VERGE computes a verification score aggregating individual claim scores with variance-based penalty to guide iterative refinement.
VERGE outperforms Proof of Thought on open-domain and linguistically ambiguous datasets like AR-LSAT, though PoT remains dominant on ProofWriter.
The SMT solver's unsatisfiable core extraction capability is used as the basis for computing Minimal Correction Subsets.
Autoformalization uses semantic equivalence checking to achieve consensus among multiple candidate logical formulas.
The formalization barrier describes the scale threshold above which LLMs can reliably produce valid SMT code for formal verification.
Vikash Singh is the lead author who introduced the VERGE framework.
Amazon Web Services is the primary research institution where VERGE was developed.

papers.title: Agent Contracts: A Formal Framework for Resource-Bounded Autonomous AI Systems
papers.summary: This paper introduces Agent Contracts, a formal framework that governs autonomous AI agents through explicit resource and temporal constraints, defined as a seven-tuple C=(I,O,S,R,T,Φ,Ψ). The framework extends the Contract Net Protocol metaphor from task allocation to resource-bounded execution, establishing conservation laws for multi-agent delegation hierarchies. Empirical validation across four experiments demonstrates 90% token reduction, 525× lower variance in iterative workflows, and zero conservation violations in multi-agent settings.

papers.title: Adaptive Stopping for Multi-Turn LLM Reasoning
papers.summary: This paper proposes MiCP (Multi-Turn Language Models with Conformal Prediction), the first conformal prediction framework designed for multi-turn LLM reasoning pipelines such as adaptive RAG and ReAct. MiCP allocates distinct error budgets across reasoning turns to enable principled early stopping while maintaining formal coverage guarantees. Experiments on five QA benchmarks show MiCP achieves target coverage while reducing turns, inference cost, and prediction set size.

papers.title: Compiled AI: Deterministic Code Generation for LLM-Based Workflow Automation
papers.summary: This paper introduces 'compiled AI', a paradigm where large language models generate executable code artifacts once during a compilation phase, after which workflows execute deterministically without further model invocation. The approach is evaluated on function-calling (BFCL) and document intelligence (DocILE) tasks, demonstrating 57x token reduction, 450x latency improvement, and 40x cost reduction compared to runtime inference. The system is particularly suited to high-stakes enterprise workflows such as healthcare, where reliability, auditability, and compliance are regulatory requirements.

papers.title: A Formal Framework for Uncertainty Analysis of Text Generation with Large Language Models
papers.summary: This paper introduces a formal framework for measuring uncertainty in LLM-based text generation by modeling prompting, generation, and interpretation as interconnected autoregressive processes combined into a sampling tree. The framework introduces filter functions to select relevant parts of the sampling tree and objective functions to incorporate syntactic or semantic considerations into uncertainty estimation. The authors demonstrate how existing uncertainty quantification approaches can be expressed within this unified framework and identify unexplored aspects such as interpretation uncertainty and systematic estimation method interactions.

papers.title: Towards Real-World Industrial-Scale Verification: LLM-Driven Theorem Proving on seL4
papers.summary: This paper proposes AutoReal, an LLM-driven theorem proving method for real-world industrial-scale formal verification, specifically targeting the seL4 microkernel verification project in Isabelle/HOL. AutoReal introduces two key improvements: chain-of-thought (CoT)-based proof training and context augmentation, instantiated in a 7B-parameter model called AutoReal-Prover. The approach achieves a 51.67% proof success rate on 660 seL4 theorems, substantially outperforming the prior best of 27.06%, and generalizes to security-related Archive of Formal Proofs projects with a 53.88% success rate.

papers.title: Efficient PRM Training Data Synthesis via Formal Verification
papers.summary: FoVer is a framework that synthesizes Process Reward Model (PRM) training data from formal reasoning tasks by annotating step-level error labels using formal verification tools (Z3 and Isabelle), eliminating the need for costly human annotation or repeated LLM calls. The resulting dataset, FoVer-40K, is used to fine-tune LLMs as PRMs, and experiments on 12 benchmarks demonstrate that these PRMs improve performance not only on math and logic tasks but also on diverse informal reasoning tasks like NLI and BBH, demonstrating formal-to-informal transfer and cross-task generalization.

papers.title: ProbGuard: Probabilistic Runtime Monitoring for LLM Agent Safety
papers.summary: ProbGuard is a proactive runtime monitoring framework for LLM agents that anticipates safety violations through probabilistic risk prediction using Discrete-Time Markov Chains (DTMCs) learned from execution traces. The framework abstracts agent states into symbolic representations, applies PAC-style statistical guarantees, and intervenes when predicted risk of reaching unsafe states exceeds a user-defined threshold. Evaluated on autonomous driving and embodied household agent domains, ProbGuard provides warnings up to 38.66 seconds ahead of violations and reduces unsafe behavior by up to 65.37% while preserving 80.4% task completion.
papers.citation:
ProbGuard: Probabilistic Runtime Monitoring for LLM Agent Safety
Haoyu Wang [0009-0000-6379-5312](https://orcid.org/0009-0000-6379-5312 "ORCID identifier") Singapore Management UniversitySingapore [haoyu.wang.2024@phdcs.smu.edu.sg](https://arxiv.org/html/2508.00500v3/mailto:haoyu.wang.2024@phdcs.smu.edu.sg), Christopher M. Poskitt [0000-0002-9376-2471](https://orcid.org/0000-0002-9376-2471 "ORCID identifier") Singapore Management UniversitySingapore [cposkitt@smu.edu.sg](https://arxiv.org/html/2508.00500v3/mailto:cposkitt@smu.edu.sg), Jiali Wei [0000-0003-1842-0196](https://orcid.org/0000-0003-1842-0196 "ORCID identifier") Xi’an Jiaotong UniversityChina [weijiali1119@stu.xjtu.edu.cn](https://arxiv.org/html/2508.00500v3/mailto:weijiali1119@stu.xjtu.edu.cn) and Jun Sun [0000-0002-3545-1392](https://orcid.org/0000-0002-3545-1392 "ORCID identifier") Singapore Management UniversitySingapore [junsun@smu.edu.sg](https://arxiv.org/html/2508.00500v3/mailto:junsun@smu.edu.sg)
(2026)
Abstract.
Large Language Model (LLM) agents increasingly operate across domains such as robotics, virtual assistants, and web automation. However, their stochastic decision-making introduces safety risks that are difficult to anticipate during execution. Existing runtime monitoring frameworks, such as AgentSpec, primarily rely on reactive safety rules that detect violations only when unsafe behavior is imminent or has already occurred, limiting their ability to handle long-horizon dependencies. We present ProbGuard, a proactive runtime monitoring framework for LLM agents that anticipates safety violations through probabilistic risk prediction. ProbGuard abstracts agent executions into symbolic states and learns a Discrete-Time Markov Chain (DTMC) from execution traces to model behavioral dynamics. At runtime, the monitor estimates the probability that future executions will reach unsafe states and triggers interventions when this risk exceeds a user-defined threshold. To improve robustness, ProbGuard incorporates semantic validity constraints in the abstraction and provides PAC-style guarantees on the learned model under standard assumptions. We evaluate ProbGuard in two safety-critical domains: autonomous driving and embodied household agents. Across evaluated scenarios, ProbGuard consistently predicts traffic law violations and collisions in advance, with warnings up to 38.66 seconds ahead of occurrence. In embodied agent tasks, ProbGuard reduces unsafe behavior by up to 65.37% while preserving up to 80.4% task completion. ProbGuard is implemented as an extensible open-source runtime monitor integrated with the LangChain agent framework and introduces minimal runtime overhead.

papers.title: A Framework for Formalizing LLM Agent Security
papers.summary: This paper presents a contextual security framework for LLM agents that formalizes execution context through four security properties: task alignment, action alignment, source authorization, and data isolation. The authors introduce oracle functions that define the theoretical information required to verify these properties, then use the framework to redefine existing attacks (indirect prompt injection, jailbreak, task drift, etc.) as violations of specific properties. The framework systematizes existing defenses as approximations of oracle functions and reveals fundamental limitations in context-agnostic approaches.

papers.title: VERGE: Formal Refinement and Guidance Engine for Verifiable LLM Reasoning
papers.summary: VERGE is a neurosymbolic framework that combines Large Language Models with SMT solvers to produce verification-guided answers through iterative refinement. The system decomposes LLM outputs into atomic claims, autoformalizes them into first-order logic, and verifies logical consistency using automated theorem proving with three key innovations: multi-model consensus via formal semantic equivalence checking, semantic routing for different claim types, and precise error localization via Minimal Correction Subsets (MCS). Evaluated across six reasoning benchmarks, VERGE achieves an average 18.7% performance uplift at convergence compared to single-pass approaches.
